moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing the Bird Opening, an unconventional yet sophisticated approach where we immediately stake a claim in the center and open lines for our kingside pieces. By advancing the f-pawn, we control e5 and g5, prevent Black from playing ...e5 easily, and prepare to support a potential e4 advance—all while keeping our position flexible and avoiding the heavily analyzed main lines. This opening appeals to players who want to steer the game toward positions where understanding and creativity matter more than pure memorization.

Black immediately plays the provocative Horsefly Defense, an offbeat response that challenges our setup by preparing ...g6 and ...Bg7 while bringing the knight to the rim—a square that looks awkward but actually supports Black's dark-squared bishop and prevents us from easily expanding with e4. This move signals that Black is avoiding main-line theory and hoping to exploit the flexibility of our f4 opening; we should respond calmly with our own plan rather than being lured into attacking a piece that has legitimate purpose.
